preamble: The complete elision of the velar stop [k] is the final stage of a lenition chain that typically moves from stop to fricative to glide before disappearing entirely.

examples.note: Finnish consonant gradation: strong grade /k/ → weak grade ∅. E.g., 'puku' (suit) → 'puvun' (GEN) — though that's k→v. A cleaner k→∅: 'suku' → 'suvun' shows k→v; pure k→∅ occurs e.g. 'jokea' → 'jo-' in compounds.
Indonesian nasal prefix meN-: initial /k/ is deleted and replaced by the nasal /ŋ/ of the prefix (KPST rule: k, p, s, t deleted after nasal prefix).
Complete elision of the intervocalic velar stop.
Loss of the final velar consonant over time.

In Western Nuer dialects, word-final /k/ undergoes lenition to approximant [ɰ] or complete elision [∅], distinguishing Western Nuer from the Jikany-based standard variety which retains /k/. This represents a synchronic or recent historical sound change marking dialect divergence within the Nuer speech community.
